Category: Education & Public Sector: Education, Government Services, Non-Profit organizations
Description: Risks in resource allocation and impact assessment.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with how your non-profit organisation operates, including general awareness of budgeting and programme planning, is all you need to get started with this course.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key risks associated with using AI in resource allocation and explain how these risks can affect decision making within non-profit organisations. You will also be able to apply practical frameworks to assess the impact of AI-driven decisions, helping your organisation prioritise ethical and effective outcomes.

lessons.Description: This lesson introduces non-profit professionals to the ways artificial intelligence can support and complicate decision making within their organisations. You will explore how AI tools are being used to allocate limited resources more efficiently and to assess the impact of programmes and services. Along the way, we will examine the real risks that come with relying on AI, including issues around bias, data quality, and accountability. By the end of this lesson, you will have a clearer understanding of how to approach AI thoughtfully, so that it serves your mission rather than undermining it. No prior technical knowledge is required, just an openness to exploring how these tools might apply to your work.
lessons.content: Risks in resource allocation and impact assessment.

slides.subtitle: Exploring AI risks in non-profit decisions
slides.slidecontent:
• AI tools are increasingly used by non-profits to guide decisions on funding, staffing, and programmes
• This course focuses on the risks
• Poor AI decisions can misdirect limited resources and harm the communities you serve
• No prior technical knowledge required
• Real examples drawn from resource allocation and impact assessment challenges

slides.subtitle: Why non-profits face unique challenges
slides.slidecontent:
• Limited resources make every allocation decision critical
• Vulnerable populations depend on accurate assessments
• Historical data often reflects existing inequalities
• Non-profit staff may lack technical expertise to spot problems
• Accountability is harder when algorithms influence decisions

slides.subtitle: When funding decisions go wrong
slides.slidecontent:
• Biased training data consistently underfunds certain communities
• Automated systems lack human context for real-world needs
• Over-reliance on historical patterns misses emerging issues
• Small data errors compound into large misallocations
• Geographic or demographic blind spots emerge systematically

slides.subtitle: Measuring what matters most
slides.slidecontent:
• Quantitative metrics overshadow qualitative outcomes
• AI struggles to capture beneficiary wellbeing and satisfaction
• Programme success gets reduced to incomplete data points
• Long-term community impact becomes invisible to algorithms
• Staff misinterpret AI confidence scores as absolute truth

slides.subtitle: Recognising patterns that skew results
slides.slidecontent:
• Consistent recommendations favouring certain demographics
• Rural, minority, or under-resourced communities repeatedly overlooked
• Results that appear neutral but embed structural inequalities
• Training data that doesn't represent your actual beneficiary population
• Historical funding patterns perpetuated without justification

slides.subtitle: Understanding your foundation and responsibility
slides.slidecontent:
• Incomplete or outdated data produces unreliable recommendations
• Missing context about local conditions and community needs
• Unclear responsibility when AI-influenced decisions cause harm
• Difficulty explaining algorithmic choices to stakeholders
• Staff overconfidence in apparently precise AI outputs

slides.subtitle: Practical steps for safer AI use
slides.slidecontent:
• Regular auditing of AI outputs for fairness across groups
• Frontline staff review of recommendations before implementation
• Cross-checking AI suggestions against beneficiary feedback
• Documentation of all overrides and decision rationale
• Scheduled reviews to update models as needs change

slides.subtitle: Your next steps with AI tools
slides.slidecontent:
• Start with small pilots to build expertise gradually
• Maintain human oversight of all AI-influenced decisions
• Establish clear accountability chains and decision documentation
• Build diverse review teams to catch different types of errors
• Create feedback loops from beneficiaries and frontline staff

scripts.script: Welcome, and thank you for joining this course on AI in non-profit decision making. Over the past few years, AI tools have found their way into all sorts of organisational decisions, and non-profits are no exception. From allocating funding to managing staffing and designing programmes, these tools are being used in ways that can have a real impact on the communities you serve. Now, this course is not here to celebrate that trend. We are going to focus specifically on the risks, because when AI gets things wrong in a non-profit context, the consequences can be serious. Limited resources get misdirected, and the people who depend on your organisation can end up worse off. The good news is that you do not need any technical background to follow along. Everything will be explained in plain language, and we will be drawing on real examples from areas like resource allocation and impact assessment. So let us get started.

Before we dive into specific risks, it's important to understand why non-profits face particular challenges with AI. Unlike private companies that can absorb some inefficiency, every pound or dollar matters when you're working with limited resources. Your decisions directly affect vulnerable people who may have nowhere else to turn. This creates enormous pressure to get things right. Adding to this challenge, most AI systems learn from historical data, and in the non-profit sector, that data often reflects decades of systemic inequalities. Finally, many non-profit staff lack the technical background to spot when an AI tool might be producing questionable results. This combination of high stakes, biased data, and limited technical expertise creates a perfect storm for AI-related problems.

Let's examine what can go wrong specifically with resource allocation. When AI tools are trained on historical funding data, they often learn to replicate past inequalities. If rural communities were historically underfunded, the AI may continue that pattern, presenting it as an objective recommendation. The algorithms also struggle with context that humans take for granted. They can't factor in a sudden economic downturn in one area or recognise when a community's needs have fundamentally shifted. Small errors in your data can compound over time too. If you misclassify one programme's success rate, that error influences future recommendations, creating a ripple effect that grows worse with each funding cycle.

Impact assessment presents its own set of challenges with AI. The most significant issue is that AI excels at processing numbers but struggles with the human stories behind those numbers. A programme might show modest statistical improvements while transforming participants' sense of hope and community connection. AI systems miss these qualitative outcomes entirely. They reduce complex human experiences to data points, potentially undervaluing programmes that create the most meaningful change. There's also a danger that staff begin treating AI confidence scores as gospel truth. When an algorithm assigns a programme a 73% effectiveness rating, that precise number can seem very authoritative, even though it may be based on incomplete or inappropriate measures.

One of the most important skills for any non-profit using AI is learning to spot bias in the outputs. The tricky thing is that biased results often don't announce themselves obviously. Instead, you need to look for patterns over time. If AI recommendations consistently favour urban over rural areas, or certain ethnic groups over others, that's a red flag worth investigating. Sometimes the bias is more subtle, embedded in apparently neutral recommendations that actually perpetuate structural inequalities. A good starting point is examining whether the training data accurately represents the communities you serve. If your beneficiaries aren't well represented in that historical data, the AI's recommendations won't serve them well either.

Beyond bias, there are fundamental questions about data quality and accountability that every non-profit must address. AI systems are only as good as the information they receive, and non-profit data is often incomplete, inconsistent, or outdated. Local conditions, community relationships, and cultural factors that influence programme success rarely make it into databases. This creates a gap between what the AI knows and what actually matters for effective service delivery. There's also the thorny question of responsibility. When an AI-influenced decision leads to poor outcomes, who is accountable? The software vendor? The staff member who implemented the recommendation? These accountability gaps can create serious legal and ethical problems down the line.

Now let's talk about concrete steps you can take to reduce these risks. The most important habit is regular auditing of AI outputs, specifically looking for patterns of unfair distribution across different community groups. Don't let AI make final decisions alone, bring your frontline staff into the process because they'll often spot problems the algorithm cannot see. Cross-check AI recommendations against your organisation's own impact records and beneficiary feedback, as your organisation holds institutional knowledge that no model can replicate. Whenever you override an AI recommendation, document why you made that choice, this creates valuable evidence for future decisions. Finally, establish regular review cycles so your models stay current as community needs evolve.

As you move forward with AI tools, remember that successful adoption is about building capabilities gradually and responsibly. Start with small pilot projects where you can learn without major consequences. This might mean using AI to inform one type of decision while keeping everything else manual for now. Always maintain clear human oversight, with designated staff responsible for reviewing and approving AI recommendations. Document your processes thoroughly so you can explain decisions to stakeholders and learn from both successes and mistakes. Build diverse review teams because different perspectives catch different types of problems. Most importantly, create feedback mechanisms so beneficiaries and frontline staff can flag issues you might otherwise miss. The goal isn't to eliminate all risk, but to use AI thoughtfully in service of your mission.

Who is this for: Programme managers, executive directors, development officers, grants managers, fundraising coordinators, operations managers, impact assessment specialists, non-profit administrators
Team or Dept this is for: Programme Management, Operations, Development, Executive Leadership, Strategic Planning
